answer:
The real-time voice assistant had three main parts: Twilio for voice streaming, a Node.js backend for orchestration, and a React frontend for the kiosk UI.

When the patient or doctor spoke, Twilio captured the audio stream and sent it to our backend over WebSocket. The backend forwarded audio chunks to the GPT realtime API and received streaming response events back from the model.

On the frontend, React showed the live consultation state: listening, thinking, speaking, and completed. Text responses were streamed into the UI as they arrived, and audio playback was handled through the browser audio layer.

The important part was lifecycle management. We had to open the WebSocket only when a session started, keep the same connection alive during the consultation, handle disconnects gracefully, and close everything when the session ended.

Simple flow: patient speaks -> Twilio streams audio -> Node backend forwards chunks -> GPT realtime responds -> React updates text/audio state -> doctor reviews structured output.

Strong interview line: I treated the realtime assistant as a streaming system, not a normal request-response API.

The hardest part was managing streaming state and interruption handling at the same time.

With a normal API call, the frontend sends one request and waits for one response. With GPT realtime APIs, audio chunks, partial text, speech events, tool events, and playback events arrive continuously. The UI has to stay synchronized with all of those moving parts.

The first challenge was connection stability. If the WebSocket dropped, we had to show the correct state, retry safely, and avoid duplicating listeners or opening multiple connections.

The second challenge was partial responses. The model might stream half a sentence, then update or continue it. React state had to append chunks carefully without overwriting newer data.

The third challenge was interruption. If a user spoke while the assistant was still talking, the app had to stop current playback, clear the queued response, and switch back to listening immediately.

Strong interview line: The difficult part was not calling the API; it was controlling a realtime conversation loop with clean state, cleanup, and recovery.

In the zero-code platform, the frontend acted like a rendering engine. Instead of hardcoding every screen, React rendered workflows, forms, validations, and actions from configuration data supplied by the backend.

The backend would send a JSON schema describing what should appear on the page: component type, labels, fields, validations, permissions, default values, and API actions. The frontend mapped that schema to reusable React components.

For example, if the schema said `type: text_input`, the renderer picked the text input component. If it said `type: approval_step`, it rendered the approval module with the right fields and actions.

This made the platform flexible because non-developers could create or modify workflows without asking frontend developers to ship a new screen every time.

The hard part was keeping it safe. We had to validate schema shape, handle missing configuration gracefully, and prevent one broken module from crashing the entire page.

Strong interview line: The frontend was not just displaying pages; it was interpreting configuration and turning it into usable enterprise workflows.

We managed dynamic forms with schema-driven rendering. The backend returned field definitions, and React mapped those definitions to actual components.

A field definition included things like field type, label, placeholder, default value, validation rules, visibility conditions, and dependency rules. For example, a dropdown selection could decide whether another field should appear.

The frontend form engine looped through the schema and rendered the correct component for each field. Text fields, date pickers, dropdowns, file uploads, and nested sections all came from the same rendering pattern.

The hardest part was conditional validation. A field might be required only when another field has a specific value. We handled that by evaluating validation rules against the current form state instead of treating all fields as static.

To keep performance stable, we avoided re-rendering the full form on every change. We memoized field components and updated only the affected sections.

Strong interview line: Schema-driven forms let the backend control form structure while the React app handled rendering, validation, and user experience consistently.

I used TanStack Query because server state is different from local UI state. API data needs caching, retries, loading states, stale-data handling, background refetching, and request deduplication.

Before using it, teams often write the same boilerplate repeatedly: `isLoading`, `error`, `data`, retry logic, manual cache updates, and refetch functions. TanStack Query gives all of that in a structured way.

For example, on a dashboard page, if multiple components need the same workflow data, TanStack Query can reuse one cached response instead of firing duplicate API calls. If the user returns to the tab, it can refetch in the background and keep the UI fresh.

It also helped with mutations. After creating or updating something, we could invalidate the related query and let the screen refresh with the latest server data.

I still used Zustand or Redux for client-side UI state. TanStack Query was specifically for server state: data fetched from APIs.

Strong interview line: TanStack Query removed a lot of custom API-state code and made caching, refetching, retries, and stale data behavior predictable.

I optimized React rendering by first identifying the actual bottleneck instead of adding memoization everywhere blindly.

The first tool was React DevTools Profiler. It showed which components were re-rendering, how often they rendered, and how expensive each render was.

The most common fixes were component splitting, React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, and better state placement. If only one small panel needed state, I kept that state local instead of putting it in a global store that caused wider updates.

For large lists, I used virtualization so the browser rendered only visible rows instead of hundreds or thousands of DOM nodes.

For drag-and-drop or canvas-heavy screens, I avoided pushing every mouse movement through React state. In those cases, refs, selective store subscriptions, or transient Zustand updates were better.

I also used lazy loading for heavy routes and components so the first page load did not download everything at once.

Strong interview line: My rule is profile first, then optimize the render path that actually hurts the user.

The biggest API issues in enterprise apps were large payloads, inconsistent response shapes, slow endpoints, and race conditions between concurrent requests.

Some APIs returned deeply nested objects with far more data than the screen needed. Rendering directly from those nested payloads made components complex and slow, so we normalized the data into flatter structures before putting it into state.

Another issue was inconsistent responses. One endpoint might return `null`, another might omit the field, and another might return an empty array. The frontend had to defend against all of those cases to avoid crashes.

Timing was also a problem. If the user changed filters quickly, older API responses could arrive after newer ones and overwrite the correct state. We solved that with AbortController, request IDs, and query libraries like TanStack Query.

For slow APIs, I used loading skeletons, pagination, caching, and background refetching so the UI stayed usable while data was loading.

Strong interview line: Enterprise frontend work is often about making imperfect APIs feel reliable and predictable to the user.

Race conditions happen when multiple async operations finish in a different order than expected. In React, this often appears in search, filters, dashboards, or workflow screens.

For example, a user types `react`, and the request for `rea` might return after the request for `react`. If we blindly set state with every response, the older response can overwrite the newer result.

I handled this in three ways. First, I used AbortController to cancel stale fetch requests when the input changed or the component unmounted. Second, I used request tracking IDs so late responses were ignored if they did not match the latest request. Third, in screens using TanStack Query, query keys helped isolate requests by parameter.

I also made sure cleanup functions were present in useEffect so old async work did not update unmounted components.

Strong interview line: I do not assume the latest request will finish last; I explicitly cancel or ignore stale responses.

Accessibility work in healthcare was important because the UI had to be usable by different people in busy clinical environments, sometimes under stress.

I focused first on semantic HTML: real buttons for actions, labels connected to form fields, meaningful headings, and proper navigation structure. This gives keyboard and screen-reader support before adding ARIA.

For keyboard access, every control had to be reachable with Tab, operable with Enter or Space where appropriate, and visible with a clear focus ring.

For forms, I made validation errors readable and connected them to the fields they described. In healthcare workflows, an unclear form error can slow down a consultation.

For custom UI like modals or dropdowns, I handled focus management: trap focus inside the modal, close on Escape, and restore focus to the trigger after closing.

Strong interview line: Accessibility was not just compliance; it made the clinical workflow faster, safer, and less error-prone.

In Agile sprint planning, my role was to break frontend work into clear tasks, estimate effort, identify dependencies, and make sure the team understood the technical risks before committing.

For a feature, I usually split work into UI components, API integration, state management, validation, error handling, testing, and deployment checks. That made estimates more realistic than giving one big number for the whole feature.

I also coordinated with backend and QA. If the frontend depended on an API contract, I made sure the request/response shape was agreed early. If QA needed test data or feature flags, we planned that before the sprint started.

For multiple product streams, I watched for shared-component conflicts. If two teams needed the same component, we clarified ownership and sequencing so they did not block each other.

Strong interview line: I used sprint planning to reduce surprises, not just to assign tickets.

My production debugging process starts with reproducing the issue and narrowing the scope.

First, I check what changed recently: deployments, feature flags, API changes, config changes, or browser-specific issues. Then I try to reproduce the bug with the same user role, environment, and data shape.

In the browser, I inspect console errors, failed network calls, request payloads, response bodies, storage, and performance traces. If the issue is state-related, I use React DevTools to inspect props, state, and render behavior.

For production-only bugs, logs and tracing are important. I correlate frontend timestamps with backend logs to see whether the failure came from the browser, network, API, or data layer.

Once I identify the cause, I prefer a small focused fix, then add a regression test or monitoring signal if the bug could happen again.

Strong interview line: I debug production issues by moving from symptom to boundary: browser, network, API, state, and deployment history.

The biggest difference is that enterprise apps are built around scale, permissions, workflows, auditability, and long-term maintainability.

In a normal app, the main goal may be building screens quickly. In an enterprise app, the same screen might behave differently for different roles, clients, regions, and environments.

Permissions are a major difference. A user may see only certain actions based on their role, and the backend must enforce the same rules.

Integrations are another difference. Enterprise apps often connect to internal APIs, third-party systems, reporting tools, authentication providers, and legacy data sources.

Reliability also matters more. A small frontend bug can block a business process, so error handling, logging, testing, deployment controls, and rollback plans become part of the architecture.

Strong interview line: Enterprise frontend development is less about isolated screens and more about building reliable workflows that survive scale, permissions, and change.

If you opened my codebase, the first thing you would notice is modular structure. I try to keep features, shared components, API logic, and state management clearly separated.

Shared UI components would be reusable and predictable: buttons, inputs, modals, tables, cards, and form controls would have consistent props and styling patterns.

API calls would usually be centralized in a service layer instead of scattered across components. That makes error handling, authentication headers, and response normalization easier to manage.

State would be scoped carefully. Local UI state stays inside components, feature-level state can live in Zustand, and complex shared workflow state can use Redux or another structured store.

For enterprise systems, I also like configuration-driven workflows where possible. Instead of hardcoding every form or workflow step, the app can render parts of the UI from schema.

Strong interview line: The main architectural decision is separation of responsibilities: components render, services talk to APIs, state stores coordinate data, and schemas drive configurable workflows.
